did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper describes a retrospective cohort study where 2,849 dermoscopic images of skin tumors were evaluated by a machine learning (ML) system to determine eligibility criteria for ML analysis. The study specifically addresses limitations in using ML for melanoma diagnosis, noting that only 36.6% of melanomas were acceptable for analysis by state-of-the-art ML systems due to factors like lack of surrounding normal skin or pigmentation.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'ORIGINAL' in the header and describes a retrospective cohort study involving 2,849 dermoscopic images. It presents original methods, results (e.g., 36.6% of melanomas were acceptable for ML analysis), and conclusions regarding ML limitations, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Uso del aprendizaje automático en el diagnóstico del melanoma. Limitaciones por superar
brief_summary: This retrospective cohort study evaluated the limitations of using machine learning for melanoma diagnosis by assessing the eligibility of dermoscopic images for ML analysis. The researchers collected 2,849 high-quality dermoscopic images of skin tumors from 2010 to 2014 and classified them based on ML inclusion criteria. Among the 528 melanomas analyzed, only 36.6% were deemed acceptable for state-of-the-art ML systems, while 63.4% were excluded. The primary causes for exclusion were the absence of surrounding normal skin (40.5%) and lack of pigmentation (14.2%). The study concludes that future ML systems must be trained on larger, more representative datasets reflecting routine clinical practice to overcome these current limitations.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study specifically analyzed 528 melanomas from a cohort of 2,849 dermoscopic images of skin tumors to determine eligibility for machine learning analysis. 36.6% were acceptable, while 63.4% were excluded.

The study included 2,849 high-quality dermoscopic images of skin tumors collected between 2010 and 2014. While melanoma was the specific focus of the eligibility analysis, the broader dataset consisted of various skin tumors.

all_task_types.details: The study did not train a model to predict a diagnostic label (e.g., melanoma vs. benign) or a continuous clinical outcome. Instead, it retrospectively evaluated the eligibility of 2,849 dermoscopic images for machine learning analysis based on inclusion criteria (e.g., presence of surrounding normal skin, pigmentation). The task was to determine which images met the criteria for ML processing, resulting in a classification of 'acceptable' (36.6%) vs 'excluded' (63.4%) for ML analysis, but this was an evaluation of dataset limitations rather than a predictive modeling task for diagnosis.

datasets_used.name: Hospital Clínic de Barcelona Dermoscopic Image Cohort
datasets_used.description: A retrospective cohort of high-quality dermoscopic images of skin tumors collected between 2010 and 2014 from the Dermatology Service at Hospital Clínic de Barcelona, Spain.

use.other: The dataset was used to evaluate the eligibility of dermoscopic images for machine learning analysis based on inclusion criteria (e.g., presence of surrounding normal skin, pigmentation). It served as the source for assessing limitations in ML application rather than for training or testing a diagnostic model.

architecture_details.description: The paper mentions 'state-of-the-art ML systems' and 'last-generation ML systems' in the context of evaluating image eligibility, but does not specify a particular model architecture (e.g., CNN, ResNet, VGG) or system name used for the analysis.

training_details.description: The study was a retrospective cohort evaluation of image eligibility, not a model training study. No training schedule, optimizer, or loss function is reported for a specific model.

conclusions: Only 36.6% of melanomas in the study cohort were considered acceptable for analysis by state-of-the-art machine learning systems.
The primary limitations for machine learning analysis were the absence of surrounding normal skin (40.5% of melanomas) and the absence of pigmentation (14.2%).
Future machine learning systems must be trained on larger, more representative datasets that reflect routine clinical practice to overcome current limitations.
Many of these limitations are being overcome by recent advances in the scientific community.
important_remarks: Only 36.6% of melanomas in the study cohort were deemed acceptable for analysis by state-of-the-art machine learning systems, indicating a significant limitation in current ML applicability.

The primary limitation for ML analysis was the absence of surrounding normal skin, which excluded 40.5% of melanomas, highlighting a bias in current ML inclusion criteria towards images with specific framing.

A substantial portion of melanomas (63.4%) were excluded from ML analysis, primarily due to lack of surrounding normal skin (40.5%) and absence of pigmentation (14.2%), suggesting current ML systems are not robust to common clinical variations.

The study relies on a retrospective cohort from a single center (Hospital Clínic de Barcelona) collected between 2010 and 2014, which may limit the generalizability of the findings to other populations or more recent clinical practices.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals, relying instead on descriptive statistics to characterize eligibility rates.

No specific machine learning model architecture or algorithm was tested or detailed; the study evaluates eligibility against 'state-of-the-art' systems generally rather than validating a specific model.

The dataset consists of high-quality dermoscopic images, which may not fully represent the variability and lower quality of images encountered in routine clinical practice, potentially underestimating the difficulty of real-world ML deployment.
